preamble: Retroflexion of the dental stop [t] to [ʈ] or [tʂ] occurs through assimilation to a preceding retroflex consonant, or through rhotacism in Sino-Tibetan languages where r-clusters (e.g., tr- or -r clusters) develop into retroflex stops. This is a major phonetic process in both Indo-Aryan and Tibetic languages.

examples.from: Classical Tibetan t- and tr- consonant clusters
examples.to: Modern Lhasa Tibetan ʈ [tʂ] or ʈʰ [tʂʰ]
examples.note: Historical consonant clusters with r (e.g., tr-, thr-) developed into retroflex affricates. The subjoined r in Classical Tibetan orthography marks the historical rhotacization process: tʂ/tʂʰ.

examples.from: Classical Tibetan written subjoined -r (e.g., tshogs troop)
examples.note: The subjoined r was historically a retroflex-conditioning element, creating the modern retroflex affricate series in Lhasa Tibetan.
Dental stop becoming retroflexed by preceding [ʂ] (e.g., in past participles like 'iṣṭa' [ɪʂʈə] from 'ish + ta').
RUKI rule: sibilant-triggered retroflexion of *d → ḍ. The preceding sibilant [ʂ] caused retroflexion of the following consonant.

sources: Masica, C. P. (1991). The Indo-Aryan Languages. Cambridge University Press.
Fortson, B. W. (2010). Indo-European Language and Culture: An Introduction.
